fieldRecord.observationNote: Fill this block first when adding a new field observation. The website uses these values for the location card and the interactive locality mini-map.

media.caption: Young fruit body of Witches cauldron in moss and conifer litter.
Close view showing the dark fertile disc and velvety brown outer surface.

hero.lede: A striking spring fungus of older conifer forest, Witches cauldron is one of the most memorable fungi you can encounter in Scandinavia: dark, heavy, velvet-brown outside, and filled with a gelatinous reservoir that gives the fruit body its odd, cauldron-like form.
hero.shortMorphology: Large barrel- to globe-shaped fruit bodies, usually 5–12 cm wide, with a glossy black-brown disc and a velvety chocolate-brown outer surface. Older fruit bodies flatten and wrinkle as they mature.
hero.returnReason: This is a species worth checking repeatedly through spring because fruiting varies strongly among years and can track local habitat quality and disturbance.

taxonomy.resolvedAgainstDataset: Catalogue of Life checklist via GBIF name_backbone(checklistKey=...)

quickFacts.label: Primary habitat
quickFacts.value: Older spruce-dominated forest with moss and conifer litter

quickFacts.label: Main fruiting window
quickFacts.value: Late March to May in Sweden

distribution.globalSummary: A boreal to hemiboreal fungus best known from northern Europe, especially Sweden, Finland and parts of Russia, with much sparser records elsewhere.
distribution.swedenSummary: Sweden is one of the species' global strongholds, with many historical and current reports concentrated in suitable spruce-forest landscapes.

notes: The map shows aggregated GBIF occurrence density rather than individual points.
Filters are restricted to Human Observation and Machine Observation records from year 2000 onwards and to records with occurrenceStatus = PRESENT.

distribution.samplingBiasNote: GBIF maps reflect reporting and digitization effort as much as biological reality. Sparse regions can mean under-sampling rather than absence.

internationalContext.summary: Globally, Witches cauldron is a conspicuous but declining forest fungus whose best-documented strongholds are in northern Europe. It is associated with older spruce-dominated forests, fruits very early in the season, and is sensitive to habitat degradation.
ecology: Usually described as a litter-associated terrestrial fungus in older spruce forest; the exact trophic mode is still debated.
Fruits shortly after snowmelt, often in moss and conifer litter on well-drained but moist ground.
Abundance fluctuates strongly between years, so repeated visits are often needed even at known localities.
threats: Clear-cutting and loss of long-continuity older forest habitat.
Overgrowth and habitat densification where historical forest grazing has ceased.
Broader forest damage and habitat-quality decline across parts of the range.

phenology.northernEurope: Often March to May, shortly after snowmelt.
phenology.note: Timing varies with winter conditions and local snow cover.

swedishContext.summary: In Sweden, bombmurkla is a nationally important conservation species and one of the country's best-known spring fungi. Swedish sources emphasise older spruce forest, long forest continuity, and the need to revisit known sites even in years without visible fruit bodies.

redList2025.documentation: Artens levnadssätt är okänt. Den bildar fruktkroppar i mossa och barrförna på sandblandad mark i äldre blåbärs- och ängsgranskog, ofta vid moränryggar och i närheten av bäckdråg. Arten är välkänd och ofta eftersökt, med många nu försvunna lokaler. De senaste årens granbarkborreangrepp har försämrat statusen på flera av artens växtplatser i kärnområdet. Med förväntat ökade skogsskador i Götaland och Svealand bedöms hotnivån stiga. Sverige hyser en stor del av artens globala population. Antalet reproduktiva individer skattas till 17000 (11000-34000). Antalet har skattats genom att multiplicera tre faktorer: 1) Troliga antalet aktuella lokaler i landet (850). 2) Bedömt antal unika mycel/lokal (2). 3) En schablon för hur många fragmenterade enheter varje unikt mycel bedöms ge upphov till (10). Antalet lokalområden i landet skattas till 850 (550-1700). Utbredningsområdets storlek (EOO) överskrider gränsvärdet för rödlistning. Förekomstarean (AOO) skattas till 3300 (2250-6600) km². En minskning av populationen pågår eller förväntas ske. Minskningen avser förekomstarea, kvalitén på artens habitat och antalet lokalområden. Minskningstakten har uppgått till 30 % under de senaste 50 åren. Under de kommande 50 åren förväntas minskningstakten uppgå till 50 %. Under en tidsperiod om 50 år, som sträcker sig både bakåt och framåt i tiden, så bedöms minskningstakten uppgå till 40 %. Bedömningen baseras på minskad geografisk utbredning och/eller försämrad habitatkvalitet. De skattade värdena som bedömningen baserar sig på ligger alla inom intervallet för kategorin Starkt hotad (EN). Minskningstakten överstiger gränsvärdet för Starkt hotad (EN) enligt A-kriteriet. (A3c).

protectedStatus: Nationally protected in Sweden; the species must not be picked or damaged.
keyFacts: Artfakta is the primary Swedish species-information source to expand this entry, with taxonomy, species information, conservation context and maps on the Bombmurkla taxon page.
Naturvårdsverket's action programme describes the species as tied to older, nutrient-rich spruce forest with long continuity and notes February–May fruiting.
SLU Artdatabanken's Bombmurkleuppropet highlights that site revisits and non-detections are valuable because fruiting varies strongly between years.

artfakta.note: Use Artfakta as the main Swedish species-information source when refreshing this page locally.

reportingCall.title: Bombmurkleuppropet 2026
reportingCall.summary: SLU Artdatabanken asks observers to revisit known sites, report both findings and non-findings, and document current site condition.
